question_text: The rock-cut architecture represents one of the most important sources of our knowledge of early Indian art and history. Discuss. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Rock‑cut architecture (caves, temples, monasteries) provides durable, datable evidence of ancient Indian art, religion, and society.
Chronological record: From Barabar caves (Mauryan) to Ajanta (Gupta) to Ellora (Rashtrakuta) – shows evolution of styles and patronage.
Religious history: Buddhist chaityas and viharas, Hindu (Elephanta, Mahabalipuram), Jain (Ellora) reveal coexistence and shifts.
Artistic knowledge: Paintings (Ajanta) depict courtly life, dress, and foreign relations; sculptures show craftsmanship.
Inscriptions: Donor records (e.g., Nasik caves) inform economic and political history.
Thus, rock‑cut architecture is an invaluable primary source for reconstructing early Indian art, culture, and history.

question_text: Pala period is the most significant phase in the history of Buddhism in India. Enumerate. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Pala dynasty (8th–12th century CE) – last great patrons of Buddhism in India.
Established great monastic universities: Nalanda (revived), Vikramshila, Odantapuri, Somapura.
Developed Vajrayana (Tantric Buddhism) which spread to Tibet, Nepal, and Southeast Asia.
Patronage of Buddhist art – Pala school of sculpture (black stone) and illustrated manuscripts.
Diplomatic and cultural missions to Tibet (Atisha Dipankara) – made Tibetan Buddhism.
Protected Buddhism from early Islamic invasions (until fall of Pala).

question_text: Evaluate the policies of Lord Curzon and their long term implications on the national movement. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Lord Curzon (Viceroy 1899–1905) pursued authoritarian policies that inadvertently fueled Indian nationalism.
Partition of Bengal (1905) – divided on religious lines, sparked Swadeshi Movement and boycott of foreign goods.
Indian Universities Act (1904) – tightened control over colleges, but led to student activism.
Calcutta Corporation Act – reduced Indian representation, united moderates and extremists.
Ancient Monuments Act – preserved heritage but also created a sense of shared cultural pride.
Long‑term: Partition revoked in 1911, but the Swadeshi momentum led to formation of Muslim League (1906) and radicalised Congress (Split at Surat).
Curzon’s policies transformed the national movement from elite petitioning to mass agitation with swadeshi and boycott as tools.

question_text: Discuss the geophysical characteristics of Circum-Pacific Zone. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Circum‑Pacific Zone (Pacific Ring of Fire) is a 40,000 km horseshoe shaped belt of intense tectonic activity.
Plate boundaries: Convergent boundaries – subduction of Pacific Plate under continental plates (e.g., Nazca under South America).
Volcanism: 75% of world’s active volcanoes – e.g., Mt. Fuji, Mt. St. Helens, Krakatoa.
Earthquakes: 90% of world’s earthquakes – e.g., 1960 Chile (Mw 9.5), 2011 Japan (Mw 9.0).
Tsunamis: Generated by undersea quakes – 2004 Indian Ocean (though not in ring, pattern similar), 2011 Japan.
Trenches: Deepest oceanic trenches – Mariana Trench, Tonga Trench.
Mountain building: Andean and Cascade ranges, Japanese Alps.
Thus, the Ring of Fire is the world’s most seismically and volcanically active region, shaping landscapes and hazards.

question_text: The process of desertification does not have climatic boundaries. Justify with examples. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Desertification is land degradation in arid, semi‑arid and dry sub‑humid areas. It is not limited to hot deserts; human activities cause it even in non‑arid zones.
Example: Mediterranean region (Spain, Italy) – overgrazing and deforestation have created man‑made deserts (e.g., Monegros).
Example: Loess Plateau, China – unsustainable farming turned fertile land into degraded land; restoration needed.
Example: Sahel region (semi‑arid) – overgrazing and climate variability caused desertification, though it borders Sahara.
Example: Punjab (India) – intensive agriculture and groundwater depletion cause salinisation, a form of desertification in a humid region.
Example: Aral Sea basin – irrigation diversions created a desert on former lakebed (arid climate but human‑induced).
Thus, desertification occurs wherever human pressure exceeds land’s carrying capacity, regardless of climatic boundaries.

question_text: How will the melting of Himalayan glaciers have a far-reaching impact on the water resources of India? (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Himalayan glaciers feed major rivers (Ganga, Indus, Brahmaputra). Accelerated melting due to climate change will drastically alter water availability.
Short‑term: Increased meltwater causes glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs) and higher summer flows, leading to flooding.
Long‑term: Glacier retreat reduces dry‑season flow. Many north Indian rivers become seasonal, affecting irrigation and drinking water.
Impact on hydropower: Reduced and erratic flow will affect hydroelectric projects (e.g., NHPC projects).
Groundwater recharge: Many alluvial aquifers depend on glacier melt; depletion will worsen water scarcity.
Transboundary issues: Reduced flow to Pakistan and Bangladesh may heighten water conflicts.
Melting glaciers threaten India’s water security – from floods now to droughts later, requiring adaptive water management.

question_text: Account for the present location of iron and steel industries away from the source of raw material, by giving examples. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Traditionally, iron and steel plants were located near raw materials (coal, iron ore). Modern plants are often located near markets or ports due to changed economics.
Example: Japan – no significant iron ore or coal, yet world’s 2nd largest steel producer. Plants located at coastal ports (e.g., Kimitsu, Fukuyama) for imported ore and coal.
Example: South Korea – POSCO at Pohang and Gwangyang, relying entirely on imported raw materials.
Example: India – Visakhapatnam Steel Plant (Vizag) located at port, uses ore from Bailadila (Chhattisgarh) and coking coal imported.
Reasons: Availability of cheap sea transport, economies of scale, coastal power plants, and proximity to consuming industries (automotive, shipbuilding).
Thus, location of steel industry has shifted from raw material to market/port orientation due to globalisation and logistics.

question_text: Has caste lost its relevance in understanding the multi-cultural Indian Society? Elaborate your answer with illustrations. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Caste remains a lived reality in India, though its forms have changed. It is not yet irrelevant for understanding society.
Political relevance: Caste-based voting, reservation policies, and political mobilisation (e.g., Yadav, Jat, Dalit parties).
Social relevance: Marriages largely endogamous; caste associations and caste panchayats still function.
Economic relevance: Caste correlates with occupation (manual scavenging still exists), wealth, and land ownership.
However, urbanisation, education, and inter-caste marriages have reduced its rigidity. Example: Metro cities see anonymity and mixing.
Illustration: Attacks on inter-caste couples (e.g., khap panchayats) show caste violence persists.
Caste has not lost relevance; it has transformed and remains a key lens for inequality and identity in multicultural India.

question_text: COVID-19 pandemic accelerated class inequalities and poverty in India. Comment. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: The pandemic disrupted livelihoods unevenly, widening pre‑existing class divides and pushing millions into poverty.
Informal workers: Migrants lost jobs, faced reverse migration without wages – no safety nets. Middle and upper classes worked from home.
Digital divide: Online education and work favoured those with devices and internet; poor children dropped out.
Healthcare access: Rich could afford private hospitals; poor faced overcrowded public facilities.
Data: Pew Research – 32 million middle‑class Indians fell into lower income; 75 million became poor.
Government relief (Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Yojana) helped but insufficient to prevent increased inequality.
Thus, COVID‑19 acted as an inequality accelerator, exposing structural vulnerabilities and deepening class divides.

question_text: Do you agree that regionalism in India appears to be a consequence of rising cultural assertiveness? Argue. (Answer in 150 words)

points.text: Regionalism has multiple causes – economic deprivation, political neglect, and also cultural assertiveness.
Cultural assertiveness: Demand for separate states based on language (Telangana, Vidarbha) or ethnicity (Bodoland, Gorkhaland).
Example: Punjab – Anandpur Sahib Resolution demanded autonomy based on Sikh identity and cultural rights.
Example: Tamil Nadu – Dravidian movement asserted Tamil cultural identity against Hindi imposition.
However, economic factors are equally strong: backward regions (Kalahandi, Marathwada) demand separate states for development.
Thus, regionalism is not solely cultural; it is a mix of cultural pride and economic grievance.
I partially agree. Cultural assertiveness is a significant trigger, but regionalism is ultimately a politico‑economic phenomenon.

question_text: Indian philosophy and tradition played a significant role in conceiving and shaping the monuments and their art in India. Discuss. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Indian monuments are not mere structures; they embody philosophical concepts (cosmology, devotion, renunciation) and traditional aesthetics.
Buddhist philosophy (anicca, anatta) – Stupas represent the cosmos and the Buddha’s parinirvana. Chaityas imitate wooden architecture but symbolise the path to enlightenment.
Hindu temple – conceived as a cosmic mountain (Meru) with garbhagriha as womb of universe. Shikhara represents ascending spiritual planes. Mandala layouts (Vastupurushamandala) embed sacred geometry.
Jain tradition – emphasis on austerity and tirthankaras: Dilwara temples with intricate marble carving depicting celestial realms.
Islamic monuments – though not originally Indian, they absorbed local traditions: Dome as symbol of divine vault, calligraphy as art of the word.
Philosophical influence: Advaita’s unity reflected in Khajuraho’s erotic art (sublimation of desire). Bhakti movement led to simpler, devotional structures (Vitthala temple, Pandharpur).
Traditional texts (Shilpa Shastras, Vastu Shastras) provided precise rules for proportions, orientation, and iconography.
Thus, Indian monuments are tangible expressions of philosophy and tradition, making art a vehicle for spiritual and cultural values.

question_text: Persian literary sources of medieval India reflect the spirit of the age. Comment. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Persian became court language under Delhi Sultanate and Mughals. Its literary output (chronicles, poetry, letters) mirrors the political, social, and cultural ethos of medieval India.
Chronicles (Tarikh) – e.g., Ziyauddin Barani’s *Tarikh‑i‑Firuz Shahi* reflects the ruling class’s view of statecraft, orthodoxy, and anxiety over Hindu subjects.
Mughal official histories – Abu’l Fazl’s *Akbarnama* and *Ain‑i‑Akbari* show the spirit of imperial ideology, centralisation, and syncretism (Din‑i‑Ilahi).
Autobiographies – Jahangir’s *Tuzuk‑i‑Jahangiri* reveals the emperor’s personal interests (art, nature, justice) and the age’s cultural refinement.
Persian poetry (Ghazal, Masnavi) – e.g., works of Amir Khusrau and Faizi reflect composite culture, love, and Sufi mysticism (spirit of harmony).
Administrative manuals and letters (Insha) – give insight into revenue systems, court etiquette, and diplomatic relations (e.g., *Chahar Gulshan*).
Limitations: Mostly elite, court‑centred; rarely voice of common people or non‑Muslims. Yet they are indispensable for understanding political events and cultural syncretism.
Thus, Persian sources capture both the grandeur and contradictions of medieval Indian polity, reflecting the composite spirit of the age.

question_text: Since the decade of the 1920s, the national movement acquired various ideological strands and thereby expanded its social base. Discuss. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: The 1920s marked a turning point: Gandhian mass mobilisation, socialist ideas, and communal politics diversified the freedom struggle.
Gandhian ideology: Non‑cooperation (1920), Civil Disobedience (1930) brought peasants, workers, women, and urban poor into movement – expanded social base beyond educated elite.
Socialist strand: Congress Socialist Party (1934) – Jayaprakash Narayan, Nehru. Advocated class struggle and economic equality, attracting youth and trade unions.
Communist strand: Peasant movements (Bihar, Bengal, Telangana) – radical land rights agenda, mobilised rural poor and tribals.
Communal ideologies: Muslim League (demand for separate electorates, later Pakistan) mobilised Muslim middle class; Hindu Mahasabha (Hindutva) appealed to upper‑caste Hindus.
Regional and caste‑based strands: Justice Party (Madras) and non‑Brahmin movements brought lower castes; Akali Dal in Punjab.
Thus, the movement became multi‑class and multi‑ideological, though also fragmenting along communal lines.
The 1920s‑30s saw the national movement evolve from elitist to mass‑based, incorporating diverse ideologies that both strengthened and divided it.

question_text: The interlinking of rivers can provide viable solutions to the multi-dimensional inter-related problems of droughts, floods and interrupted navigation. Critically examine. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: National River Linking Project (NRLP) proposes transferring water from surplus to deficit basins to mitigate floods and droughts and enable navigation.

Potential benefits: Drought mitigation in water‑scarce regions (e.g., linking Ganga to Cauvery). Flood control by diverting excess water. Year‑round navigation (Ganga‑Brahmaputra link) could boost trade.
Criticisms: Environmental – disruption of river ecology, loss of forests, submergence of land. Social – displacement of millions without adequate rehabilitation.
Financial and technical: Estimated cost > ₹15 lakh crore; inter‑state disputes over water sharing (e.g., between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu).
Alternatives: Local rainwater harvesting, aquifer recharge, efficient irrigation, and desalination are more cost‑effective and less destructive.
International implications: Linking Ganga to Cauvery would affect Bangladesh (Farakka dispute already tense).
While conceptually attractive, river interlinking is fraught with ecological, social, and political costs. A cautious, piecemeal approach with rigorous EIA is needed, not a grandiose plan.

question_text: Account for the huge flooding of million cities in India including the smart ones like Hyderabad and Pune. Suggest lasting remedial measures. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Flooding in million‑plus cities (Chennai, Hyderabad, Pune, Mumbai) has become annual, despite smart city branding.
Reasons: Unplanned urbanisation – encroachment on lakes, wetlands, and riverbeds (Hyderabad’s Musi river, Pune’s Mula‑Mutha).
Poor drainage: Inadequate stormwater systems, garbage choking drains, and conversion of permeable surfaces to concrete.
Climate change: Extreme rainfall events (cloudbursts) exceed design capacity of infrastructure.
Topography: Pune on foothills, Hyderabad on undulating terrain – water flows into low‑lying areas.
Remedial measures: Restore and protect natural water bodies (e.g., Hyderabad’s lakes revival project).
Improve drainage with larger capacity and regular desilting; separate stormwater from sewage.
Adopt sponge city concepts (permeable pavements, rain gardens, green roofs) – tried in China.
Enforce building codes and flood zoning; no construction in floodplains.
Early warning systems and flood shelters; integrated stormwater management plans.
Lasting solutions require political will to reverse encroachments and invest in climate‑resilient urban infrastructure.

question_text: India has immense potential of solar energy though there are regional variations in its development. Elaborate. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: India receives ~300 sunny days a year, solar potential > 750 GW. Yet deployment is uneven across states.
High potential regions: Thar Desert (Rajasthan, Gujarat) – high solar radiation and vast land; leading in installed capacity (Bhadla Park).
Peninsular India: Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh – good radiation, plus government policies and private investment.
Low development regions: Northeast India – high cloud cover, hilly terrain, and lack of transmission infrastructure; limited large‑scale projects.
Northern plains (UP, Bihar) – despite adequate radiation, land fragmentation and population density hinder utility‑scale solar; rooftop solar slower.
Reasons for variation: Land availability, state policies (net metering, subsidies), industrial demand, and investor confidence.
Potential: Rooftop solar in cities (Delhi, Mumbai) – underutilised. Floating solar in reservoirs (MP, Kerala) – emerging.
India’s solar future requires addressing regional barriers – grid integration, land acquisition, and financing – to unlock full potential uniformly.

question_text: Examine the status of forest resources of India and its resultant impact on climate change. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: India’s forest cover (21.67% of geographic area as per ISFR 2019) is increasing but quality and density are concerns.
Status: Very dense forest (only 2.9%), moderately dense (9.6%), open forest (9.1%). Recorded forest area > 23% but much under non‑forest use.
Challenges: Deforestation due to mining, infrastructure, and encroachment. Forest fragmentation reduces biodiversity.
Positive trends: Increase in forest cover (since 2015) due to plantation and conservation schemes (CAMPA, Green India Mission).
Impact on climate change: Forests act as carbon sink – India’s carbon stock ~7,000 million tonnes. Deforestation releases CO2.
Impact on microclimate: Forest loss leads to local temperature rise, reduced rainfall, and increased desertification (e.g., Aravalli degradation).
Impact on vulnerability: Degraded forests increase flood and drought risk (Western Ghats).
India’s NDC target: Create additional 2.5‑3 billion tonnes carbon sink through forest cover by 2030.
While forest cover is increasing, quality improvement and reducing deforestation pressure are critical to mitigate climate change.

question_text: Is diversity and pluralism in India under threat due to globalisation? Justify your answer. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Globalisation brings both homogenising pressures and opportunities for cultural revival. Its impact on Indian diversity is complex.
Threats: Westernisation of lifestyle – fast food, English dominance, and consumer culture erode local traditions (dress, cuisine, festivals).
Media and entertainment: Global streaming (Netflix, Amazon) reduce audience for regional language content, though they also produce local originals.
Economic globalisation: Uniform branding and mall culture threaten small artisans (handloom, handicrafts).
Counter‑trends: Globalisation also revives traditional knowledge (yoga, Ayurveda) as global wellness trends.
Diaspora and digital media spread Indian culture globally (Bollywood, classical music) – creating pride and preservation.
Political backlash: Globalisation-induced inequality has fuelled regional and caste‑based movements that assert local identity.
Thus, globalisation is a double‑edged sword – it threatens certain local practices but also provides tools for cultural preservation and hybridity.
Diversity is under selective threat, not extinction. With conscious policies (e.g., GI tags, language protection), pluralism can survive globalisation.

question_text: Customs and traditions suppress reason leading to obscurantism. Do you agree? (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Customs are inherited practices. When enforced rigidly without questioning, they can suppress rational inquiry and promote obscurantism (resistance to knowledge).
Examples of suppression: Caste‑based untouchability – no rational basis, yet enforced by custom, blocking social justice for centuries.
Sati (widow burning) – a custom justified by tradition, abolished by reformists using reason (Raja Rammohan Roy).
Female genital mutilation (Dawoodi Bohra) – defended as custom, condemned by medical science and human rights.
Superstitions (witch‑hunting, animal sacrifices) – flourish where customs discourage scientific temper.
However, not all customs are obscurantist. Many embody accumulated wisdom (e.g., seasonal festivals linked to agriculture, dietary rules based on climate).
Reason can coexist with tradition when customs are reinterpreted rationally (e.g., green Ganesha, eco‑friendly Holi).
Thus, the problem is not custom per se, but dogmatic adherence that rejects questioning.
I agree that customs, when divorced from reason, lead to obscurantism. A healthy society continuously subjects traditions to rational scrutiny.

question_text: How have digital initiatives in India contributed to the functioning of the education system in the country? Elaborate your answer. (Answer in 250 words)

points.text: Digital initiatives under Digital India, NEP 2020, and especially during COVID‑19, have transformed education delivery, access, and management.
SWAYAM platform: Massive open online courses (MOOCs) from school to postgraduate level, free access to quality content.
DIKSHA (Digital Infrastructure for Knowledge Sharing): e‑content for teachers and students, used across states for remote learning.
PM e‑VIDYA: Integrated platform combining TV (Swayam Prabha channels), radio, and online classes – reaching students without internet.
Virtual Labs: Remote access to experiments for science and engineering students, reducing infrastructure gaps.
NAD (National Academic Depository): Digital storage of academic certificates, reducing forgery and enabling easy verification.
Challenges: Digital divide (only 24% rural households have internet), lack of devices, and teacher training in remote pedagogy.
Positive impacts: Continuity of education during lockdowns, exposure to global courses, and data‑driven governance (UDISE+).
Digital initiatives have made education more accessible and resilient, but bridging the digital divide is essential for inclusive benefit.
